input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin offers simulation, analysis, and optimization services, delivering real‑time insights, predictive analytics, and decision‑support capabilities for the physical asset throughout its lifecycle.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The approach enriches BIM models with device definitions (sensors, actuators, controllers), creating a unified semantic foundation. Model extraction follows Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) to produce structured representations that are automatically transformed into deployable Digital Twin artifacts. These artifacts comprise the geometric BIM information together with behavioral descriptors of the defined devices, and are stored in the DT platform for runtime synchronization.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators are modelled by the Building Control Engineer directly within the BIM environment. The DT framework represents these actuators as executable control points that can be invoked remotely by the Facility Manager through the twin interface, enabling command of physical hardware in the building. No specific actuator types or actuation ranges are detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are likewise defined in the enriched BIM model by the Building Control Engineer. The DT extracts their semantic description and maintains a real‑time data stream from the physical sensors to the twin, allowing the Facility Manager to monitor measured values. The document does not specify sensor categories, sampling rates, or transmission characteristics.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Real‑time data exchange provides continuous synchronization of the physical building’s operational state to its digital counterpart. The modular architecture and standardized extension points enable integration of sensor and device data streams, allowing ongoing ingestion of physical asset information into the digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated in the digital twin are sent back to building actuators through the framework’s bidirectional communication channels. Runtime configuration management validates and applies these commands without requiring full redeployment, and the digital twin receives feedback confirming execution of the physical actions.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The digital twin system is built on a modular, service‑oriented architecture with standardized extension points that expose network interfaces for component interaction. This design supports interoperability across heterogeneous subsystems and scalability from single devices to whole infrastructure, while runtime configuration management allows dynamic adjustment of connections to maintain reliability.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework supports a full DT lifecycle that begins with device modeling inside BIM (defining sensors, actuators, and controllers), proceeds to model‑driven engineering where structured representations are extracted via Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE), then automatic generation of deployable DT artifacts, followed by deployment and runtime operation with real‑time data exchange and interactive visualization, and finally evolution through modular extensions via standardized extension points. Lifecycle governance is implied by the model‑driven approach that maintains a single source of truth from BIM through to the running twin.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: DT creation follows a model‑driven engineering (MDE) process: building‑control engineers enrich BIM models, the framework extracts a formal representation, and code generators produce deployable twin services. The modular architecture with standardized extension points allows DT engineers to add custom services, analytics, or plugins. Evolution is managed by updating the BIM model and regenerating artifacts, preserving traceability between model changes and twin updates. Automated generation and continuous runtime synchronization provide implicit quality assurance, though specific testing or version‑control practices are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin offers decision support through continuous real‑time data synchronization and immersive interactive visualizations that let facility managers monitor system states and issue control commands. Insights are presented via dashboards and 3D visual contexts reflecting current sensor readings and actuator statuses, enabling operational decisions; the documents do not specify confidence metrics or advanced analytics.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework builds on Building Information Modeling (BIM), an established industry standard for representing building geometry and assets, and extends it by standardising BIM semantics for control system components (sensors, actuators, controllers). It introduces standardized extension points within its modular architecture to enable interoperable integration of custom services, analytics, and external tools. While no external certification schemes or specific interoperability standards (e.g., IFC, ISO 19650) are cited, the approach aligns with industry practice by using BIM as the canonical data source and providing a uniform, standards‑based integration layer for Digital Twin extensions.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the document's description of the model-driven approach. It correctly identifies the enrichment of BIM with device definitions, the use of MDE for extraction, and the composition of the resulting artifacts. Quotes: "This enables building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a unified semantic foundation", "The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract structured representations from these enriched BIM models and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts", "Starting with a BIM model which contains all relevant information pertaining deployment... after validating the model... the model is fed into the Hono/Ditto Configurator".

The description is well-supported by the text regarding the modeling of actuators in BIM and their representation in the DT as control points for the Facility Manager. It correctly notes the absence of specific actuator types or ranges. Quotes: "define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment", "Facility Managers who monitor and control building operations", "interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control".

Matches the text's description of sensors being defined in BIM and real-time data streaming to the twin for the Facility Manager. It correctly identifies the lack of specific technical details in the document. Quotes: "define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment", "Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts", "Facility Managers who monitor and control building operations".

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The provided text mentions an evaluation metric called the 'Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE)' to measure how well the twin reflects its physical counterpart, but it does not detail model fidelity requirements, verification methods, uncertainty sources, or error correction mechanisms as requested by the characteristic description. Therefore, the 'Not in Document' claim is justified.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies real-time data exchange and synchronization as key mechanisms, supported by the source's mention of 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange' and 'enabling live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins'. It also correctly links the modular architecture to data ingestion, citing 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'. It captures the system's intent well, though it omits specific protocol names.

The extraction accurately describes the bidirectional nature of the system, supported by quotes like 'bidirectional communication between virtual and physical assets' and 'mechanisms to feed control signals back to the BCS'. However, the claim that 'Runtime configuration management validates and applies these commands' is an inference not strongly supported by the text, which states runtime configuration 'enables adjustments without requiring full redeployment' rather than validating specific control commands. Thus, it lacks some technical precision regarding validation.

The description of a 'modular, service-oriented architecture with standardized extension points' is directly supported by the text: 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'. The mention of interoperability and scalability aligns with the document's stated requirement for 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems' and contribution supporting 'scalable... development'. It accurately reflects the architectural design described in the sources.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', and the source text does not contain the specific infrastructure details required by the characteristic definition. While the text discusses a 'model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment' (Abstract) and mentions software tools (Eclipse Hono, Ditto, Unreal Engine), it does not specify hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational and storage requirements, or specific deployment models.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

However, it does not provide details on data ownership policies, privacy protection measures, consent management, or data anonymization specific to the Digital Twin system.

The provided text describes a framework for 'model-driven deployment' and 'real-time data exchange' using technologies like Eclipse Hono and Ditto. It focuses on technical challenges such as 'heterogeneous models' and the 'absence of standardized integration frameworks'. There is no mention of cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, or safety-critical considerations in the provided excerpts.
